nativeRange.habitat: The Honeycomb Coral is generally reported from protected fringing reefs, back reef slopes, and atoll lagoons, and tends to favor sheltered, slightly silty environments without strong wave action.

decorPreferences: open exposed ledge with clear surroundings
several inches of spacing from neighboring corals

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Has been reported to benefit from supplemental weekly target feedings of mysis shrimp, finely chopped meaty foods, oyster-feast, and micro-plankton. Feeding tentacles are typically extended at night; targeted feeding in the evening is generally most effective.

goodWith: other LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: corals placed within sweeper tentacle reach (~10 cm)
aggressive SPS corals in direct contact

summary: The Honeycomb Coral (Diploastrea heliopora) is a slow-growing LPS coral prized for its striking, uniform honeycomb surface. Generally considered hardy and forgiving once established, it suits intermediate reef keepers with stable tanks. It extends sweeper tentacles at night and needs clear spacing from neighboring corals.
careNotes:
TAXONOMIC NOTE: Diploastrea heliopora is the sole species in the genus Diploastrea and was reclassified into its own family Diploastreidae following molecular studies. Older aquarium literature still places it in Faviidae or Merulinidae — those references are outdated. It is NOT interchangeable with Favia or Favites despite a superficially similar honeycomb pattern: Diploastrea corallites are notably more uniform, slightly raised, and dome-shaped, with a single, distinctive central columella. It is the only living member of its family.

In the aquarium trade, Diploastrea is typically sold as frags or small colonies (~5-15 cm). Corallites are approximately 1 cm in diameter, closely packed, and uniformly spaced — the eponymous honeycomb appearance. Coloration in trade includes green, gold/tan, and rarer ultra-green or purple-centered morphs.

CARE OVERVIEW: Generally considered hardy and adaptable, often described as easy-to-moderate in difficulty. Once established, Diploastrea is regarded as one of the more forgiving large-polyp stony corals, though its very slow growth rate means recovery from stress events takes considerably longer than faster-growing LPS. For this reason, intermediate-level keepers with stable, mature tanks tend to do best with it; absolute beginners may be frustrated by the lack of visible growth feedback.

LIGHTING: Generally advised to keep under moderate lighting, roughly 75-200 PAR. Some sources suggest acclimating new specimens at lower intensity (50-100 PAR) and gradually increasing over 2-3 weeks. Strong direct lighting has been reported to cause bleaching.

FLOW: Moderate, indirect water flow is generally recommended. The species' natural habitat is sheltered reef slopes and lagoons, so strong laminar flow has been reported to stress the tissue. Gentle to medium flow that prevents detritus settling on the colony is ideal.

PLACEMENT: Best placed in the lower to middle portions of the aquarium on stable, exposed rockwork. Use IC gel, putty, or two-part epoxy to secure frags to a rock or ledge. Importantly, leave at least 5-10 cm of clear space around the colony — Diploastrea has been reported to extend sweeper tentacles up to ~10 cm at night to sting nearby corals.

AGGRESSION: Semi-aggressive. Nighttime sweeper tentacle extension is well documented and a common cause of stings on adjacent corals. Plan placement accordingly.

WATER CHEMISTRY: Calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium dosing is generally advised for sustained skeletal growth, though demand is modest given the very slow growth rate. Iodine, strontium, and trace element supplementation has been suggested by some sources.

FEEDING: Primarily photosynthetic. Weekly supplemental feedings of mysis shrimp, micro-plankton, or finely chopped meaty foods have been reported to support growth and color saturation. Feed in the evening when feeding tentacles are extended.

GROWTH: Notably slow-growing — one of the slowest LPS in the hobby. Some keepers report only a few millimeters of skeletal extension per year. This is part of why mature wild colonies can take centuries to reach their meter-plus dome size.
breedingNotes: Captive propagation is by fragmentation; the species' very slow growth makes fragging uncommon and recovery from cuts slow. Sexual reproduction in the wild is by broadcast spawning; deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: Reeflings - Honeycomb Coral (Diploastrea heliopora) Care Guide
primary.notes: World Wide Corals (assigned primary) returned only a generic search results page with no Diploastrea-specific product or care page; swapped primary role to Reeflings, which had the most complete care sheet. Temperature 22-26°C, pH 7.8-8.4, SG 1.023-1.026, PAR 50-200, low-moderate flow, moderate to easy care, peaceful in source language (other sources documented sweeper tentacles - see secondaries).

additional.name: LiveAquaria - Hexagon Brain Coral, Aquacultured (Diploastrea heliopora)
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters and temperament: temp 72-78°F (22-26°C), pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, dKH 8-12, moderate light, medium flow, bottom-to-middle placement, semi-aggressive with nighttime sweeper tentacle extension, weekly meaty feeding. Confirmed Diploastrea as semi-aggressive contrary to Reeflings' 'peaceful' label.

additional.name: Reeflex - Diploastrea heliopora species profile
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Diploastreidae confirmed), wild colony size (up to 2 m tall, 5 m across), depth range (3-56 m, broader than Wikipedia's 30 m), sweeper tentacle length (up to 10 cm), and tank minimum (200 L). Temperature range 25.4-29.3°C used to widen the upper bound to 27°C.

additional.name: Wikipedia - Diploastrea heliopora
additional.notes: Used for taxonomic authority: confirmed monotypic genus Diploastrea, family Diploastreidae, order Scleractinia; depth range 3-30 m on wild reefs; massive dome colonies 1 m+ across in the wild; documented as plentiful in Indonesia/Indian Ocean atoll lagoons, less common in Red Sea. Used for taxonomy and biology only; not for husbandry parameters.
